# Introduction

## Purpose

	ProjWizInc, or Project Wizard Incremental is an experimental game engine designed for educational and hobbyist purposes. The engineering objective is to understand software design principles, high-performance optimization, and decoupling strategies within simulation-heavy software. The architecture prioritizes long-term modularity, computational performance, and logical integrity.

## Vision

	Although ProjWizInc is just an engine now, the vision is to be able to use it to create a game powered by it. Initial inspiration for ProjWizInc was Orb of Creation, although later on several other games, such as Arcanum: Theory of Magic, Idle Loops, Archmage Idle and Path of Praxis, also had an impact.  
	Understandably, such a project would be quite ambitious, so several phases have been planned, and the game itself should incrementally expand

Phase 0: Current Foundational

* Jobs  
  * Standard time-based operations running on logic ticks, granting resources upon completion  
* Resources  
  * Continuous accumulation of passive resource data.  
* Json database  
  * Boot-time loading of structural configurations via dynamic JSON deserialization.  
* UI  
  * Decoupled presentation using separated frameworks (WinForms/Avalonia) to present status and process actions.

Phase 1:

* Jobs  
  * Component extensions to support multi-resource execution costs, progression curves (leveling), and requirement checks.  
* Shop  
  * Standard upgrading loops using resource expenditure.  
* Save and Load  
  * Deep save/load mechanics with automated calculations to convert offline duration into high-speed simulation ticks ("offline time").  
* UI  
  * Nested interactive tooltips (Paradox-style wiki menus).

Phase 2:

* Automation Queue  
  * Priority queue structures allowing conditional automation (e.g., execute Task A unless resource threshold is crossed, then fall back to Task B).  
* Narrative  
  * Dialogue sequencer (Visual Novel style) capable of pausing or interleaving with the underlying simulation loop.  
* Spells  
  * Active spell-casting logic mapped to the automation queue.

Phase 3:

* Combat  
  * Decoupled, automatic combat resolver utilizing 2D graphical positions and state-based AI.  
* Skills  
  * Skills and modifiers specifically applied to the combat math engine.

Phase 4:

* Items  
* Equipment  
* Crafting

Phase 5:

* Prestige/New Game+  
* Plot ending  
* Postgame Dungeon

Stretch goals:

* Homunculi  
* Summons and minions  
* Nation building

## Definitions & Glossary

* Tick  
  * Atomic unit of time for the game  
  * Everything that takes time is measured in ticks instead of time  
* Ticks Per Second (TPS)  
  * Previously called Updates Per Second(UPS)  
  * How many times per second we will perform a logic loop update  
  * Currently targeting 100 TPS  
* LongTick(int t)  
  * For some systems that dont need to be updated super frequently, they can depend on a LongTick instead  
  * LongTick(10) means that the function will run once every 10 ticks, and so on  
* Presentations Per Second (PPS)  
  * How many times per second, the game engine will update the view model  
  * Currently targeting 30 PPS, although it can go lower or higher depending on the feels  
  * Refer to the system architecture for more details  
* Frames Per Second (FPS)  
  * How many times per second the front end will refresh itself  
  * Currently using avalonia, so the game engine has no idea what the actual FPS will be like  
* ProjWizInc  
  * Full name of ProjWizInc is Project Wizard Incremental  
  * It is important to distinguish between Incremental and Incorporated, which seems to be a common confusion point  
* BigNum  
  * A custom hybrid double/scientific ADT for managing really big numbers  
  * Has a side effect of holding really small numbers as well. Small in the absolute sense rather than negative  
  * It is a readonly record struct for performance reasons, allowing it to live in stack memory rather than heap memory  
  * It is immutable, and does not even have private set to avoid boxing  
  * Constructed via a chain Master Constructor pattern, so we can just pass in any type of value, which all goes into the master constructor, which also helps avoid recursive initialization and maintain the Normalisation Invariant  
  * Current range is \+- 10 ^(+- 9.22 \* 10 ^ 18\)  
    * Thats like a number with 9e18 digits  
  * Has an epsilon of 10^-15  
    * So any number that has an exponent 15 or less than it is basically 0  
  * The internal ‘small’ value can hold integers up to 2^53, or 9e15. This means our precision is the same as integers for values up to 9e15.  
  * Because BigNum is a struct, it must use the .Equals function instead of \==. We also tolerate any differences below our epsilon  
  * Because the small is a double, if we want integer remainder behaviour, we must manually use Math.floor or % modulo  
  * Notable edge cases  
    * 0 is considered a positive number  
    * Any number larger than 1.8e308 must be built with the scientific or string constructor  
* Feature  
  * It is basically the ECS equivalent of a component  
  * Renamed as Feature to avoid namespace collision with System components  
  * Maybe we should rename it to GameComponent  
* Boot  
  * When the game engine first starts up, thats booting

# Architectural Pattern: Separated Simulation-Presentation (SSP)

## Core Architecture

This project follows an Iterative Refinement Lifecycle. We prioritise system architectural and logical integrity above all. Development cycles are characterized by identifying Architecture Spikes, solving them, then documenting them.  
This process is primarily a learning experience, and thus does not prioritise anything material. With any luck, this will mean that this project can be worked on for a long time without needing to deal with technical debt and spaghettification.  
ProjWizInc uses a hybrid Separated Simulation-Presentation (SSP) model. The physical hardware execution loop (rendering, OS event loops, visual rendering) is strictly separated from the abstract logical model (math calculation, job ticks, resource updates)  
![][image1]

## The 4 Layer Model

* Data Layer (Modder's Paradise): Read-only at runtime. Contains passive parameters, configurations, and functional components loaded from configuration files on boot.  
* State Layer (Passive Memory Containers): Dynamic memory holding runtime values. These are passive records containing no logic, ensuring simple serialization.  
* Service Layer (Logical Managers): Active, stateless services housing computational operations. They read from the Data Layer, perform math, and update the State Layer.  
* Presentation Layer (Visual Controls): Read-only viewers representing state. They poll on a throttled heartbeat, binding to the state without directly manipulating the service engines.

## Access Control & Modular Director Policies

* Encapsulation Policy: Concrete manager classes in the Service Layer are declared public to facilitate direct UI interaction. However, their constructors are declared internal to enforce the Bootstrapper as the sole Composition Root.  
* State Access Policy: State properties use public get; internal set; definitions. This guarantees that the Presentation Layer can freely read data, but only the specific Service Layer managers can write to the variables.  
* File Structure Policy: To optimize system organization, discoverability, and clean architecture compilation, each public class, interface, or struct must reside in its own dedicated .cs file matching its exact type name.

Blueprint-Instance Pattern

* To prevent the duplication of static structural data across dynamic state objects:  
* Blueprints (Definitions): Static, immutable templates loaded once into the Data Layer (e.g., JobDefinition specifying how many ticks are required to mine gold).  
* Instances (States): Lightweight, variable data tracks residing in the State Layer (e.g., JobState storing only the active ID and current tick completion progress).  
* Nomenclature Change: Component terms are normalized as "Definition Components" to eliminate structural naming conflicts with framework UI elements.

# Architectural Layers & Data Flow

## Data Layer & Serialisation Specifications

* Serialization Service & Persistence Namespace: The ProjWizInc.Core.Persistence namespace houses JSON translation structures. The SerialisationService acts as the single boundary point translating external string configurations (JSON) into internal structures.  
* Component-Based Entity Design: Blueprints divide into passive templates (DefinitionBase) and component-receptive entities (DefinitionEntity).  
  * Simple static assets (e.g., Resources) use the lightweight DefinitionBase footprint.  
  * Complex assets containing dynamic behaviors (e.g., Jobs) inherit from DefinitionEntity and compose their functionality using polymorphic components implementing IDefinitionComponentInterface.  
* Polymorphic Serialization Invariant: Components utilize polymorphic JSON serialization attributes (JsonPolymorphic, JsonDerivedType). This enables complex configurations to map string keys (e.g., "$type": "payout") to concrete C\# types like PayoutComponent.  
  * Naming Invariant: To prevent user-mod data breakage, C\# type names may undergo code refactoring, but their JSON string representation keys (e.g., "payout", "ticks") must remain constant and backward-compatible.

## Service Layer & Execution Controls

* The Service Layer houses logical engines. This layer is entirely decoupled from persistence, rendering libraries, and direct state-management code.  
* Constructor Integrity: Service Layer constructors are restricted strictly to Dependency Assignment and Event Subscription (wiring). No structural math, data operations, or cross-manager methods are permitted during construction phases.  
* Lifecycle Management: Multi-stage linking, external parsing, and complex initialization procedures are deferred to explicit setup processes called by the Bootstrapper after the complete dependency graph has been assembled.  
* Composition Bootstrapping: The Bootstrapper is the sole Composition Root. It instantiates the aggregate runtime state, resolves configuration arrays, and injects appropriate manager dependencies.  
* Reflective Register Creation: The Bootstrapper uses reflection exclusively during startup to discover lists within GameDefinitions. This ensures conformance with the Open-Closed Principle (OCP), allowing new definition categories to be added to raw JSON structures without modifying core bootstrap logic.  
* Sequential Hydration: The DefinitionManager is generated before structural resolution occurs. It serves as the single authority mapping human-readable string keys to integer index identifiers. Linkable components implementing ILinkableDefinitionInterface resolve string-based relationships into zero-allocation index IDs (e.g., caching a static index instead of executing string lookups during runtime logic loops).  
* State Separation & Communication Rules: Logical managers must remain isolated from each other. They interact solely by publishing and subscribing to events via the global EventManager. For example, the JobManager performs rewards by publishing a ResourceGainedEvent, maintaining absolute separation from the EconomyManager’s internal balance structures.  
* Manager Initialization ("Synchronize-on-Birth"): When initialized, managers must inspect their injected states. If the injected state contains existing data (e.g., a loaded save-file mapping an active ActiveJobId), the manager must immediately cache components and subscribe to appropriate systems during its construction phase. This maintains system parity between a fresh boot and a loaded game session.

## State Layer

* The State Layer acts as the runtime memory model of the engine.  
* Passive Data Containers: State classes do not contain logical routines or calculations. They contain simple arrays and value-types representing live configurations.  
* Aggregate Root: The master GameState class represents the unified root of all active memory. The SerializationService operates exclusively on this root object during save/load operations, ensuring transactional integrity of player data.  
* State Slicing Policy: To prevent cross-contamination and ensure microservice isolation, managers are never injected with the full GameState reference. Instead, they are initialized with localized slices (e.g., injecting EconomyState exclusively into EconomyManager).

# System Design Patterns & Runtime Execution

## Real-time Execution Loop

* To decouple game tick simulation from structural frame-rendering:  
* Logic Frequency: Targets 100 Ticks Per Second (TPS).  
* Presentation Frequency: Targets 30 Presentation Updates Per Second (PPS).  
* Constrained Rendering Pattern: In the event of system lag, a time accumulator processes consecutive logical updates to catch up to real-world clock time. However, to prevent UI lagging, only one Presentation update is dispatched per rendering frame, regardless of how many logical ticks occurred in the catch-up burst.  
* Rendering Interpolation: Inconsistencies between logical ticks and render frames are smoothed using interpolation. This ensures continuous visual feedback during CPU bursts, fast-forward states, or lag resolution.

## Event-Driven Architecture

* Loose logical coupling is maintained through a custom, thread-safe, array-backed EventManager.  
* To prevent GC allocation overhead in high-frequency update loops, subscriptions are managed through explicit array expansions and pointer swaps. This bypasses structural collection modification exceptions and avoids heap allocations in the critical path.

## Performance & Memory Optimization Policies

* Garbage Collector Avoidance (Heap Optimization): To maintain a stutter-free 100 TPS heartbeat, code executing in "hot paths" (e.g., logic frames, math loops) must strictly avoid heap allocations.  
* Explicit Imperative Standard: The use of var is prohibited for local variables and fields to maintain strict type contracts. The use of lambdas, short-form arrow functions (=\>), anonymous functions, and LINQ extensions in logical paths is prohibited. Standard for loops, foreach loops, and structured if/else branches must be used. This ensures complete compiler transparency, aids debugging, and minimizes dynamic delegate overhead on performance-critical threads.  
* Reflection Usage Constraints: Reflection is permitted exclusively during the bootstrapping phase. Performing reflection within active service managers, update ticks, or rendering cycles is strictly prohibited.  
* Hydration Isolation: Link resolution systems utilize IEnumerable to read references, preventing system cloning and retaining isolation boundaries. Pattern matching using the is operator is utilized to resolve types safely without exposing implementation layers.

# Technical Considerations & Domain Types

## The BigNum ADT

To manage astronomical exponential scaling, ProjWizInc utilizes a custom BigNum readonly record struct as its primary mathematical engine.  
![][image2]

* Physical Structure: Designed as an immutable readonly record struct to reside in stack memory and bypass memory fragmentation and GC overhead.  
* Mathematical Invariants:  
  * Normalization Invariant: Values in the scientific path must conform to a mantissa of 1 \<= M \< 10  
  * Overflow Invariant: Operations on the small path are validated against precision limits before execution, smoothly transitioning to the scientific path to ensure mathematical consistency.  
  * Upperbound Limit: The small path's upper boundary is set to 10^15. This guarantees IEEE 754 atomic integer precision, matching 53-bit mantissa limits.  
  * Lowerbound Limit: Decimals below 10^-4 transition to the scientific path to enforce presentation uniformity and maintain visual legibility in UI rendering layers.  
  * Sign-Mismatch Redirection: Signs are normalized prior to calculation. When adding or subtracting values of mismatched signs, the struct redirects execution to the inverse operator using unary negation (eg a \+ \-b \= a \- b) to guarantee mathematical termination.  
  * Path Transition Invariant: Transitions between small (double) and large (scientific) paths are determined strictly by the absolute magnitude of the value rather than the signed value, protecting computations from signed overflow or underflow.  
  * Precision and Scale: Employs an epsilon threshold of 10^-15 . Differences below this threshold are treated as equivalent, and the representation limits span exponents up to \+- 9.22e18 

# Quality Assurance, Testing & Validation Policies

## Testing Architecture

* We employ rigorous automated validation to prevent regression issues during logical updates.  
* Mathematical Invariant Coverage: The BigNum struct must maintain complete unit-test validation coverage for standard operations, boundaries, and sign modifications.  
* Boundary Conditions: Basic operators must be thoroughly verified against path transitions (crossing 1e-4 and 1e15) to prevent rounding errors or infinity faults.  
* Test Isolation (Test Data Policy): Unit tests must use mock and stub databases constructed dynamically in-memory (such as TestHelpers.cs).  
* Rationale: Unit tests are prohibited from loading external XML or JSON configuration files. This prevents test rot, ensuring that game balance modifications in the external defs.json cannot cause isolated logical assertions to fail. File-system validations are isolated to dedicated Integration Tests.  
* Access Configuration: Project configurations employ the InternalsVisibleTo assembly attribute. This grants the xUnitTester package direct access to internal classes, constructors, and properties, facilitating comprehensive white-box testing of the Service Layer while keeping internal engine systems cleanly insulated from the presentation layers.  
* Debugging Triangulation: In the event of system failures, developers must apply atomic triangulation testing. This isolating methodology separates logical failures into concrete Data, Service, or State bugs before writing code refactors.

# Gemini Advice

* Recommendation: Add a small "Future Considerations" note under the bootstrapping section. If Native AOT or strict trimming becomes a distribution target in Phase 1 or 2, the reflective registry parsing inside Bootstrapper may eventually need to be supplemented by a source generator or explicit registrations.  
* Example definition to include: The "hot path" is defined as any logical operation triggered within or downstream of the UpdateLogicEvent subscription (e.g., GameLoopManager.LogicStep and all manager Update methods), and any presentation rendering triggered by UpdateRenderEvent. Allocations (like reading from JSON or resizing configuration arrays) are permitted exclusively during the bootstrapping phase or game state loading phases.  
* Recommendation: Explicitly document whether presentation reads are intended to be lock-free and tolerate race conditions (which is highly acceptable for scalar values in incremental games), or if a strict synchronization policy will be implemented for non-atomic types.  
* Consolidated Partial Merging Pattern within the Data Layer and Bootstrapping sequence, which should be explicitly documented in our architecture specs.  
* Since we refactored our boot mechanism to dynamically merge lists via properties, we should update the "Bootstrapping" / "Data Layer" sections of the SADness document to note that Consolidated Partial Merging is dynamically executed via Type-Reflective IList iteration).  
* Consolidated Partial Merging Pattern dynamically mapped through type-reflective IList properties, and documented our core UI dependencies on dependency-injected view model construction.  
* Since we refactored view updates to pull on render frames and implemented Dynamic Binding Collections with a custom explicit command pattern, we should document this under the "Presentation Layer" and "System Patterns" sections.  
* If we adopt this baking system in a future phase, we should document this under the "Data Layer" and "Serialization & Persistence Layer" sections as an Asset Cooking & Dual-Path Loading Pipeline  
* (This change introduces an Independent Small-Path Constructor Entry Point pattern, which should be updated in the "BigNum ADT" section of your specifications).  
* (This introduces a Static Exponentiation Lookup Table pattern, which should be updated in the "BigNum ADT" section of your specifications).  
* (This refactor introduces the Comparison Delegation Pattern inside your BigNum ADT specifications). Instead of defining \> and \< separately, we define \< as being \! \>, which applies DRY.
